cues.subtitle: Valley Forge, Pennsylvania. Two days before Christmas, 1777. The British army is warm in Philadelphia, eighteen miles away. Washington's army — what remains of it — is here.

cues.guidance: Walk toward the first line of huts.
cues.subtitle: Twelve thousand men built this city in weeks: nearly two thousand log huts, fourteen feet by sixteen, twelve men to a hut. They built it here because there was nowhere else to go.

cues.guidance: Pause near one of the huts.
cues.subtitle: The army starves because the systems to move food have collapsed. Some nights the camp's whole ration is firecake — flour and water scorched on a stone. In the dark, the huts chant it like a taunt: no meat. No meat.
A quarter of the army will die here — not in battle, but of typhus, dysentery, and exposure. Two thousand men. Desertion runs rampant. And yet the army holds together, all winter.

cues.guidance: Stop on the parade ground and listen.
cues.subtitle: In February a Prussian officer arrived — Baron von Steuben, lately of Frederick the Great's staff. He spoke no English. He drilled them anyway, a hundred men at a time, swearing in three languages. By spring, the survivors could maneuver like professionals.

cues.guidance: Walk beyond the camp toward its far edge.
cues.subtitle: The army marched out of Valley Forge in June, into a war it would not lose. No battle was fought here; nothing was decided. The army survived the winter, and the revolution survived with it.
